sub: Why unmanned aviation needs traffic modeling at all

title: Live Flight Tracking
desc: Every airliner around you is broadcasting its position right now

body.alt: FlightAware live map with hundreds of aircraft

items: Live map of all aircraft in the sky — near you and anywhere else in the world
Positions from ADS-B transponders, radar and receiver networks; every symbol carries flight ID, altitude, speed and route
Keep this picture in mind: drones must merge into exactly this traffic
Try it yourself: flightaware.com

title: A Near Miss at Nuremberg Airport
desc: 03.10.2020 — a drone passes 'within a few meters' of a passenger aircraft

body.alt: News report: drone comes dangerously close to passenger aircraft

items: A pilot on final approach reported a sudden drone sighting at 11:00 in the morning
The multicopter hovered at several hundred meters altitude, about 8 km from Nuremberg airport
It obstructed the landing approach of an aircraft carrying 170 passengers and crew; the aircraft landed safely — only because the drone happened not to be in the flight path
This is why drone flight near airports is strictly regulated — and why we model drone traffic

body.alt: Wind tunnel test of a drone striking an aircraft wing
body.caption: University of Dayton Research Institute: a consumer quadcopter fired at an aircraft wing tears deep into the structure — unlike a bird of the same mass.

items: Drone numbers grow far faster than manned aviation — logistics, agriculture, inspection, emergency response
Low-altitude airspace over cities was never designed for traffic — no controllers, no routes, no separation services
A 1 kg drone hitting an airliner wing or engine is a structural threat, not an inconvenience
Conclusion: unmanned traffic needs its own rules, data exchange and traffic management — the subject of this lecture

title: How Are Drones Regulated Worldwide?
desc: From 'unrestricted' to outright bans — a fragmented global picture

body.alt: World map of drone regulation by country (Statista/Surfshark)

items: Regulation ranges from unrestricted (green) over line-of-sight requirements to effective or outright bans
Many countries still have no legislation at all — a problem for international operators
The EU harmonized its rules in 2021: one framework for all member states — next slides

title: The EU Drone Regulation Framework
desc: EU 2019/947 & 2019/945 — one rulebook, three risk categories, five drone classes

rows: Open
Low
Hobby & light commercial flights in subcategories A1 / A2 / A3
No authorization; class marking C0–C4, max. 120 m altitude, visual line of sight
Specific
Medium
BVLOS flights, heavier drones, urban operations (STS-01 / STS-02)
Operational authorization based on risk assessment (SORA)
Certified
High
Passenger transport, dangerous goods, large cargo drones
Certified aircraft, licensed remote pilots — like manned aviation

items: New drones are divided into 5 risk classes (C0–C4) — certified and labeled by the manufacturer
Legacy drones (built before 1.1.2024, no class marking) fall under transitional rules
Applies EU-wide, since 2023 also in Switzerland; national rules still add local specifics

title: EU Drone Regulation 2024 at a Glance
desc: Classes, altitudes, distances, insurance, registration and training in one chart

body.alt: EU drone regulation 2024 overview chart with classes C0-C6 and categories A1-A3, STS and BOS

title: Open Category: A1 / A2 / A3
desc: The three subcategories decide how close to people you may fly

rows: A1 — fly close to people
Overflight of individuals possible, never over crowds
No flight over open-air assemblies and no deliberate flight over uninvolved persons; if an overflight happens unexpectedly, it must be ended as quickly as possible
A2 — fly at a safe distance
Min. 30 m horizontal distance to uninvolved persons
May be reduced to 5 m in exceptional cases when the drone's low-speed mode is active
A3 — fly far from people
No uninvolved persons in the entire flight area
Additionally min. 150 m distance to residential, commercial, industrial or recreational areas

title: The Remote Pilot Certificate
desc: Flying legally requires proof of competency — the 'drone driver's license'

body.alt: EASA remote pilot certificate of competency card

items: A1/A3 proof of competency: online training + online exam at the national aviation authority (LBA in Germany)
A2 certificate of competency: additional theory exam — needed to fly closer to people
STS: standard scenarios in the Specific category require dedicated theory and practice
Operators must register and label their drones (e-ID); liability insurance is mandatory in Germany

title: Challenges of Air Traffic Modeling
desc: What makes drone traffic a hard modeling problem

items: Heterogeneous traffic — manned and unmanned aircraft with wildly different speeds, sizes and behavior share one airspace
Density — delivery and inspection scenarios put hundreds of drones over a single city district
Real-time data — positions, weather, geo-zones and clearances must flow with sub-second latency
Deconfliction — collisions must be predicted and avoided automatically, without human controllers
Regulation — every model must respect categories, geo-zones and altitude limits from Module 1
The rest of this lecture builds the toolbox to attack exactly these problems
